title: Trump’s extraordinary ‘settlement’ agreement is defeated by the court
gist: A Florida federal judge rejected Donald Trump's attempt to use a collusive litigation 'settlement' with the Treasury and IRS (agencies he controls) to create a $1.776bn political fund and grant himself tax immunities, ruling the maneuver was pursued in bad faith — though the author warns the underlying scheme could resurface in another form.

insights.claim: Trump structured a lawsuit-settlement between himself and his own government (the US Treasury and IRS, both under his control) to do the work of primary legislation — creating a $1.776bn fund benefiting his political allies and granting tax immunities to Trump and others, without any legislative process or court approval.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: Watch for executive-branch actors using litigation settlements as a workaround to bypass Congress and courts to create off-books funds or immunities — a governance risk pattern that could recur.
insights.quote: This one, however, was doing the job of primary legislation. Never had there been a more super-powered private contract.
insights.locator: Back in May, the Weekly Constitutional featured the extraordinary

insights.claim: Just before a court-ordered deadline to address whether a genuine dispute (and thus jurisdiction) existed, the parties had the underlying case dismissed and then tried to present the arrangement as a settlement — a procedural trick meant to place the deal beyond the court's power to review its merits.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: A template for how litigants might try to insulate self-dealing arrangements from judicial review by strategically dismissing cases before adverse jurisdictional rulings.
insights.locator: Judge Kathleen M Williams directed the parties to make submissions

insights.claim: Judge Kathleen M Williams reopened the dismissed case after former federal judges applied to the court, and found that Trump and his co-plaintiffs 'acted in bad faith and for an improper purpose' by collusively filing the lawsuit solely to provide cover for a collusive settlement, and pursued it to dishonestly advance a political narrative.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: A documented federal court finding of bad-faith litigation by a sitting president is a rare, citable precedent for future challenges to executive self-dealing schemes.
insights.quote: acted in bad faith and for an improper purpose by 'collusively filing a lawsuit with claims subject to multiple dispositive defenses solely to provide cover for a collusive settlement.'

insights.claim: As a sanction, the court barred all parties from referring to, using, or citing the purported 'settlement agreement' as evidence of a settlement in any judicial, administrative, regulatory, arbitration or other official proceeding, and referred the lawyers involved — including acting/nominated attorney general Todd Blanche — to their professional regulators.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: The $1.776bn fund and the tax immunities cannot currently be enforced or cited by Trump's side, closing off this specific legal route — for now.
insights.locator: The court then imposed as a sanction

insights.claim: The author expects the blocked fund and tax immunities could be revived in some other legal form, especially given Trump's evident demand for such arrangements and his nomination of Todd Blanche — one of the lawyers implicated in the collusive settlement — as permanent US attorney general, pending Senate confirmation.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: Investors/policy-watchers with exposure to US federal governance risk should track Blanche's Senate confirmation and any successor vehicle for the fund/immunities, since the underlying intent has not gone away.
insights.locator: This does not mean that the fund and the immunities cannot be resurrected

insights.claim: Because Trump needs the case to remain dismissed (not 'live') to preserve any settlement-based benefits, a successful appeal would revive the case and undo his position — a structural disincentive that the author believes makes this one case Trump is unlikely to pursue further on appeal.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: Reduces near-term litigation risk of the scheme reappearing via appeal in this specific case, even though the broader ambition persists.
insights.locator: He may appeal (using either side of the case)

insights.claim: The author argues that even without the specific tax immunities sought here, Trump remains 'pretty much politically immune from the consequences of his abuses of power' under the current state of US politics, implying formal legal setbacks like this one have limited deterrent effect.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: Legal wins against executive overreach may be more symbolic than substantively constraining while the political system provides cover — a reason not to overweight court rulings as a check on this administration's behavior.
insights.quote: the current nature of US politics means that even without this tax immunity he is pretty much politically immune from the consequences of his abuses of power
insights.locator: What Trump attempted here should have led to his impeachment
